narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in competitive fields like this. Your profile includes being in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, an undergraduate GPA of 2.90, an LSAT score of 34, a middle-income family background, and plans to attend full-time. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 74.3% chance that you would fail the bar exam—far higher than the lower risks seen in approved applicants. This high predicted failure risk is why your application was rejected, as the school uses these predictions to select students most likely to thrive and pass on their first try. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your third-year school decile ranking at the bottom, which stood out strongly compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack position. Your first-year decile in the lowest group was the next biggest driver in the same concerning direction, signaling to the model a consistent academic struggle below dataset averages. On a more positive note, your LSAT score, though below the average of 36.7, helped pull the prediction somewhat toward passing success. Your middle family income and full-time enrollment plans offered smaller boosts in the same favorable direction, aligning with common profiles among those who pass. Overall, your early academic deciles were the dominant forces outweighing these strengths, placing you below typical admitted students who often rank higher in school performance. While this decision stings, especially given the barriers Black women like you navigate in legal education, it's not the end of your path. I encourage you to focus on strengthening your academic record—perhaps through targeted GPA improvement or retaking the LSAT—before reapplying to build a profile that shifts the model's view. Many have turned similar setbacks into bar-passing triumphs; you have the resilience to do the same. Keep pushing forward.
